speaker: interviewer
text: --here in Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sent to that Camp Number Three. We were given a pair of shoes-- wooden shoes-- and an additional coat. When we arrived there the following day we were sent to work. As I said, I and my brother, we always stuck together. We went-- we went to work together. And he slept one next to the other one in the huts. And every morning in the dark-- in the dark of the morning, 5:00, 6 o'clock we were chased out from our hut, you know, on appellplatz. You know. And we were counted in. And we were sent one company here, and here, and here. And we all-- they were counted out-- out from the camp. And when we came back in, they counted us into the camp. Usually, I and my brother slept together. I wore my shoes, even the night-- during the night-- I wore my shoes. Either I had them on, or I put them behind my head as a pillow. My brother did not. He took his shoes off. And it so happened that one morning he got out, and he wanted to put the shoes on. And the shoes vanished. Someone stole-- some other-- some other guy stole the shoes from him. He went out, and he couldn't-- we were chased out from the hut. And they chased him out. And he was chased out barefoot. During that time, let me mention that the Germans, when they send the sick people back to Camp Number Four, or they send them back wherever they were gassed, cremated, or whatever, they took away their clothing and their shoes from them. Now, I don't know-- because he did not have the shoes, if they thought that he was sick, and he was supposed to go to the-- on the sick transport; or he felt so bad, because we were hungry, and tired, and that he said I don't care anymore, if he willingly went with the sick people because he didn't have the shoes. I don't know. When I came back at night my brother was gone. Now this was the latter part of December.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944. When I came back, my brother was gone. And since then, I never saw my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this affect how you-- how you managed--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you can-- you can imagine the effect on this one. You can imagine the effect. You know, I was-- I was just devastated. You know, I was with my brother, and now all of a sudden I am all by myself. At this point, let me go back one more thing. Before-- before, when I was still on the first slave labor near Frankfurt [GERMAN], I forgot to mention that I received-- in the ghetto-- in Lodz ghetto we could not have any mail. We did not receive, or could not mail anybody. However, in camp, we could write and receive. In that camp, I received one and only letter from my parents. This was in 1941 at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I received a letter asking me, where are you? What are you doing? Why are you there? How are you? And how is-- how is your brother? And so for. This was the only news that I received after I parted from my parents in 1940. In January of 1940. And this was the last time I heard from my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now coming back to this part, you know, this was the last time that day-- that day when someone stole my brother's shoes, this was the last time that I saw my brother. I never saw my brother again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I had to go to work there the following day. And the following day. And we were chased out. And I was, as I say, devastated. In about-- in March, we heard that Munich is about 50 miles from Landsberg, from our camps. During the night, we heard bombings. We know Munich was devastated. Devastated. They was bombed to almost nothing. Several nights-- for about three or four nights-- we saw the bombing. We saw they-- the-- either the Americans or the English when they bombed Munich, they put down what they called some [INAUDIBLE], some lights, that they will see where to bomb. And from our camp it was so light that 50 miles away we could see the light. When they were-- when they were bombing Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to see the planes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But we-- we were able-- several of them they flew over our camps. And when they flew over our camp, you know, all the camp was-- camp was dark. You know. The camp was dark, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew what it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew already what it was. However, we didn't know how close it was, and how soon it was. And we didn't know if they bombed Munich or-- or any other cities, or [GERMAN] or wherever. We didn't know that. But we heard the bombing. Now this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it give you any hope?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it gave us a little bit more courage. We'll say, maybe, maybe we'll survive. Maybe, we have a chance of surviving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was in March. And this was continuing. By the end of April-- the middle of the end of April-- they decided that they are gonna liquidate the camp. They decided that they would liquidate the camp. And they gathered us all together, several hundred of us. And they wanted to take us either to Austria or to southern Bavaria. To say-- for what reason, I don't know. They tried first. They tried to march us to Dachau. But Dachau did not want to accept us. I don't know how they knew. We traveled-- well, this is what I mentioned about the dead march. This was the dead march. We march probably about seven, eight days around. And we went around and back. And most of the marching was done during the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: During-- during the night. [INAUDIBLE] Once in a while. Once in a while, they-- because it was-- it was already a chaos. But we were watched by the SS. We were watched. And we went forth and back, forth and back during the night, because during the days they were hiding us in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say probably about seven, eight days. We were forth and back, forth and back, we were marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: How strong were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several people were shot. I couldn't walk anymore. And I fell behind. And I knew that the people who fell behind-- because we heard shots. If they shot in the air, or they shot the people, I don't know. But they were shot. And some people were shot, probably. However, on the end of the transport, they were the Germans going with dogs. And I myself fell behind. I just couldn't walk anymore. I fell behind. And the German put the dog on me. He put the dog on me. You know, I just fell. And I covered myself up with a-- with a blanket, you know, and I don't care, you know. But the German put the dog on me. And the dog bit me on the head. He bit me once on the head. And I just jumped up and ran forward to the thing. Because-- I don't know. It just-- it just was all the strength that I had. But I jumped up and I ran between the crowd. And I never wanted to be on the end again, because I knew that either the dog would get me, or they will shoot me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? We-- we were on that march seven or eight days. One morning we woke up, and there was no Germans to see around. They were just all gone. Just like vanished completely. It was, you know, a half a night we were walking, and the other half a night we were camped out in the woods. And all of a sudden, no Germans. And we started to holler and everything, and no Germans at all. German. This was May the 2nd, 1945. How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we feeled jubilant, of course. We were filled-- we hollered. And the very first thing, what we did is we ran out from the woods to the neighborhoods. And we pounded on the-- on the people's homes for food, for bread. Some people, either they were afraid, or they didn't know what goes on, or whatever. Some people gave us whatever--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you, specifically? You went to the door--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the door, and banged and-- and thing. And we felt already that that's it. We were already-- we were already liberated. You know. And they gave us some food. And we started to running on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you food? Was it a family? What was It?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. The German. Family-- they just throw it out. They gave it to us. Yes. Bread. Yes, they gave it to us. And I know [LAUGHS] that I ran around with with my packet with carrots. And one family gave us-- gave me some jam. A can-- not a can-- a jar of jam, a piece of bread. And I just stuffed my pockets. And-- and we ran on the freeways. While we were on the freeways, we notice that there are already the American--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is on the roads?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the roads, that American armies. American army vehicles. Forth and back, and riding and everything. And those were the armies that liberated Dachau itself. Now, I was-- this was near a city called Bad Toelz. It is in the vicinity from Munich, probably about 30, 40 miles in the vicinity. This is where I was liberated. Now, the Americans were smart enough to see that we should not run around like, you know. Because we were like wild, you know. We were like wild in our clothing. And [NON-ENGLISH] after anything we could see, you know, for joy or for-- or for [INAUDIBLE] or for whatever, you know. They gathered us together, and they put us on trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they talk to you? Was something speaking German?

speaker: interviewee
text: More-- more or less it was more or less like by-- by hand, you know. Because we didn't know English, and they didn't know German and thing. And more or less they gathered us up. But and they gave us cigarettes, you know, food, or whatever they gave us. And they took us to a German camp. It so happened that it was a very, very poshy camp. It was a camp from the German-- it was a [GERMAN] called [GERMAN]. It was from the high class gentleman officers, I think. And-- We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel safe with the Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yes. And we were just overjoyed. And we stayed in that camp probably about two or three days. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did someone to help you with medical, and getting you clean, and what about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ourselves, they were, as I said, it was a very high class camp. And there was plenty of water. And we showered and-- and [INAUDIBLE]. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How must that have felt to get in a hot shower?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh it was just lovely. And let me point out that at that time we also received care packages. Everyone, you know. So we could throw out the carrots already. You know. And the care packages, and the care packages. A piece of soap--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now were you able to eat right away, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Not really. Some people felt very sick. Some people felt very sick of dysentery, you know. They didn't have any food. And-- and when they gave us the food, they told us, take it easy. Don't eat this. And they were smart enough not to give us any greasy food or any fat, you know, because this is a-- you know, after-- after so many years of not eating right, it was terrible, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with any of your friends at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I was-- I made friends with the-- with some of those at the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the state of your own health and your body at the end of the war? Can you give us a description of how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --how much you think you weighed, or what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. My health was not the best health. I was missing several teeth, because some teeth were knocked out by a-- by a a German with a-- with a spade during-- during work. I couldn't perform the work, you know. So-- so he took his spade handle, and he-- he shellacked me under my chin, you know. And he knocked out a couple teeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: At which place was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in that Camp Number Three. This was in Camp Number Three. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the only time you were beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beaten-- now, at that terrible beating, yes. But a kick and a-- and a slam [INAUDIBLE] thing, this was on a daily-- this was on a daily order. But coming back to the thing. In that camp, we were given also a care package-- the first care package, you know. And we ate our full. And of course, I don't want to say that I didn't have to go 10 or 15 times to the bathroom. Probably so it was. But I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it didn't make you terribly sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, I didn't feel. The nerves-- my nerves were gone already from-- from all the years. But see, bodily, well, I could survive. I could survive bodily. We stayed in that camp in the Bad Toelz about three, four days. And, as I said, the camp was so, so luxury that the Germans-- that the Americans decided that they are going to take it for their soldiers. Which, I don't blame them. I can-- I can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us all. And we were several hundred of us in that camp. All men?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so. I believe it all men. Yes. Even in the camps, that in that Camp Number Three, where there were some women. But we were strictly separated. We never worked with them together. They were there to perform different works. We never went to work with them together. We never-- we never were together. When the Americans took over that camp, they transported us to an outskirt of Munich, to another camp. This was called-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Fuchkaserne, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Feldafing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. It was called Funkaserne-- Flak-Kaserne. Flak-Kaserne. Flak-Kaserne. And we were in that camp for about 8 to 10 days. And again, [LAUGHS] the Americans decided that they have to have that camp too, because the Americans poured in. You know. They came in now with all the vehicles, and all the power, and all the soldiers came in. And those camps were still fairly solid, you know. They were not bombed out, you know. And they took over that Flak-Kaserne too. When they took-- but in a more humane way, when they transported us, you know, they fed us and thing, you know, in a very humane way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they told you what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we knew that they were supposed to-- that they taking the camp for them. And they'll transport you to another camp. Then they gave us two choices. Where we can stay near Munich was a Funkaserne, and another camp which is called Feldafing. Now the Feldafing is roughly, probably around 25, 30 miles from Munich, where the Funkaserne is in the outskirts of Munich. However, the Funkaserne was a international camp for them. It was Russians and Poles and Greeks and Lithuanians and from every-- Jews and Gentiles-- everyone; where the Feldafing was a strictly Jewish camp. Was also from all nationalities from Jews-- Jews from Greece, from-- from [INAUDIBLE], from Lithuania, from Poland, from Hungary-- but strictly Jews. Well, of course, I don't know of course or not, but I picked Feldafing. I picked Feldafing. And I stayed in Feldafing for quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Feldafing for quite a while, for some months. Now this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do some work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We were free to do whatever we-- if you wanted to work in the kitchen, you got special clothing, you got extra clothing, or you got extra food, or fancier food, or you got another care package. But we were not-- you didn't have to work. We were free to do whatever we wanted. And we got-- we got the food in the morning. We get into the kitchen. And we got--

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you go to the town, or whatever you wanted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Now let me mention that Feldafing was again a-- a camp, a German camp, a military camp, an existing military camp, where they had everything. They had their kitchens, and-- and as a matter of fact-- as a matter of fact, in Feldafing, during the high-- during the Jewish high holiday, I don't know from where, but they got a Torah scrolls. And who would come on the-- on Yom Kippur? Then General Eisenhower came to visit us in Feldafing on Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: You bet your life. And he came, and he stayed, oh, maybe about 15, 20. Before he came, you know, there were a whole squadron of MPs, you know-- military police-- around in the-- in the camp. And he came. And he gave us a speech. 90%, probably 95%, of the people didn't know what he was talking about. He spoke in English, you know. And-- but we all listened to him. And he went around. And he shook hands with some of them. I saw him. He didn't shake hands with me. But I saw him very close. As close as-- a couple--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know who he was in terms of his role?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- no. We knew that he was a high-- a high general. But we didn't know that he was the Chief of the American Staff or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by this time, how were you feeling? Were you feeling healthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: By this time we were feeling-- yeah. And let me point out that the Germans also brought in some German-- German doctors into Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Civilian doctors?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, [BACKGROUND NOISE] one doctor that-- examined me, he was in a uniform; in a uniform, but in a very humane way. Either he probably was not an SS man. He probably was from the Wehrmacht. But Feldafing itself behind our camp there is a little village. They had a hospital. And some of them, that they were real sick, they were already transported to that-- to the village. And over there they were German doctors. And, as I said, I was examined in camp by a German doctor. And he prescribed some lamp, some heating lamps, some massages, you know. And they did not have any dentists there at that time. And I was in that camp, oh, for quite a while, probably. Til that-- til November. In November, I decided that I will go back to Lodz. Because I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find there when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- While I was in Feldafing, we went on several times to Munich to the committee to find out who was alive, and where was alive, and if they have any list. And, you know, in the Jewish committee, you know we put up our lists. I survived. I am so and so from so and so, and I survived. And anyone who knows me, or anyone who knows about my parents or my brother, just sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: You registered that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Registered there. And at that time I heard the news that some people told me, yes, I was with your brother. And I don't think so. They didn't come out and tell me exactly that he died. But I don't think so that he survived. I cannot tell you for sure, but I doubt if he survived. Now either they were too polite to tell me this, or they didn't want me to lose my hope, you know. They didn't tell me. But I had an inkling that what happened. By the end of November, I said I'm going to back to Lodz, because I knew after the war that if someone is alive from my family, we will go to Lodz to see if something, someone is there, or someone still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam, in a very summary way, tell us what happened until you got settled in a normal life again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went back. I went back to Lodz. And I registered. I was about 14 days in Lodz the first time. I registered with the Jewish committee in Lodz. And I know I met some Poles. To point out the anti-Semitism, they say-- they used to say, 'Well, they didn't kill all of you, did you? Some of you are still alive! Some of you still came back!' After seeing this, and after hearing this, you can expect how I felt. I said no, I'm not going to stay here. I went back. I-- I had to smuggle myself back, because the way to Poland, to Lodz, the Americans supplied railroad trains to go into Poland. And from Poland, you are on your own, you know. We had to go to Lodz. But on the way back, and I did not want to stay in Poland, I smuggled myself to the Czechoslovaki-- to the Czechoslovakia-- back to Germany. I stayed in Germany. I went back to Feldafing. I stayed a while still in Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were welcomed back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Feldafing there was no problem. And you know, and you didn't have to do anything. And you got your food. And you could get your bed. You know, of course, we were 10, 12 people in a-- in a room. But we were just sleeping and eating. And you were free to do whatever you wanted to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after Feldafing?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- and after being in Feldafing I decided that I want to move into-- to Munich. So I went, and I rented a room. I rented. At that time, in Munich, the [INAUDIBLE] of the-- the people who supplied, they were very, very scarce. Because you have to realize that Munich was all bombed out. There were hardly any places to be gotten. However, to the Jewish committee, and to the German policy after the war, they forced people who were Nazis, and who still had some apartment, that they had to take in some people who looked for things. I was given a room at-- at some Germans. Well, assumingly they were Nazis. Otherwise they wouldn't have forced them to take me in. They said, no, they were not Nazi. They were what they called [GERMAN]. The [GERMAN] was a German word for saying, well, I also ran. You know, I ran with the crowd. But I was not a Nazi. However--

speaker: interviewer
text: I wish we could hear these details. But we are running out of time. How-- you told me that you went to Israel. Or was it Palestine at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you able to do that? And how did that happen? And what happened there? But you need to say it in a very summary way, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went back to Poland another time in 1946, because I did not want to give up. I wanted to make sure that no one is alive. I went back to-- to Lodz again in '46. And again I spent about 14 days. I didn't find anybody alive from my family. So I left. I said goodbye. And I thought I'll never going to come back to Poland. I smuggled myself back to Eastern Germany, back to Munich, because I knew that if I want to go any place I have to go from Germany. I lived in Germany '46 til '47. In '48, I decided that I will go to Israel. While I was in-- while I lived in Munich, I worked at the Jewish committee in Munich. And in 1948, I decided I will go to Israel. By the end of 1948, you have to realize that Israel was just came to-- into existence. When I came to Israel straight from the ship, they took me from the ship that I arrived, and they put me into a boot camp-- into a military camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This wasn't your choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not my choice. I couldn't-- I didn't know what was going on. But I can realize now, and I can appreciate, they had to have all the abled men to the military, because it was either there is going to be a nation or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the service there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I were in the service close to two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any fighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not see any fighting. I was on the border, on the border, on the Syrian border. And I was on the border of Jordan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn some Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few words, yes. You have to-- you have to know a little bit Hebrew. I was in-- in the outskirt of Sembach, I believe, it was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Israel close to two years. I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I stayed [LAUGHS] in Israel. And after I got out of the Army, they were supposed to have given you some advantages-- of work, or to live. When I got out, I did not receive no place where I could stay. I could not work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I came to the Labor Department to ask for a job. They were, well, you just worked three days. You just worked a day, so how can we give you again? We have to divide. I can understand and appreciate that they didn't have to.

speaker: interviewee
text: What they wanted me is to go to a kibbutz. And a kibbutz, I don't have to tell you what a kibbutz is. A kibbutz is more or less like a camp where they work. And-- and I figured myself, my youth was lousy. A ghetto, a camp, military, now to go on a kibbutz, I don't think so. I want it. I don't want-- I want to have something for my life, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided no, I'm not going to stay there. But I did not have no passport, because I didn't have a passport. I never had a passport, because the Germans didn't give me a passport. And I didn't stay in Poland for a passport. I stayed in Israel, and I didn't have a passport. [SIGHS] And I was looking for work, and I could not get a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, after a while, I got a job on a ship. I got on a job on a ship. The ship is organization called [INAUDIBLE]. They owned the ships. I got a job on the ship as a dishwasher.
